label: The Goths
pitch:
No Germanic people did more to end the ancient world. Remembering an origin across the Baltic in the island of Scandza, the Goths moved to the Black Sea, split into Visigoths and Ostrogoths, and then did the unthinkable: at the Battle of Adrianople in 378 they killed an emperor and destroyed his army, and in 410 Alaric Sacks Rome itself — the first time the city had fallen in eight hundred years.

They were also the most cultured of the barbarians. Ulfilas Translates the Gothic Bible into a brand-new alphabet, Theodoric the Great Conquers Italy and ruled it as a Roman philosopher-king from Ravenna, and Gothic law codes preserved Roman jurisprudence. Their story runs from the Scandza legend to Justinian's twenty-year war that finally erased Ostrogothic Italy in 554.

spans.label: The Gothic Migration South
spans.description: Gothic peoples moved from the Vistula basin to the Black Sea steppe, leaving the Wielbark culture and forming the Chernyakhov culture.

spans.label: The Gothic Raids on Rome
spans.description: Gothic sea and land raids devastated the Balkans and Asia Minor and killed the emperor Decius before Aurelian stabilized the frontier.

spans.label: The Gothic War of 376–382
spans.description: Driven across the Danube by the Huns, the Goths revolted, destroyed an imperial army at Adrianople, and won a treaty as armed federates inside the empire.

spans.label: The Visigothic Kingdom
spans.description: After sacking Rome the Visigoths settled in Aquitaine and then Hispania, building a kingdom centered on Toulouse and later Toledo.

spans.label: Ostrogothic Italy
spans.description: Theodoric the Great and his successors ruled Italy as a Gothic-Roman state from Ravenna, preserving the Senate, law, and classical culture.

spans.label: Justinian's Gothic War
spans.description: The Eastern Empire's twenty-year reconquest of Italy destroyed the Ostrogothic kingdom and devastated the peninsula.

events.label: Jordanes' Scandza Origin Legend
events.description: The historian Jordanes records the Gothic tradition that the people had migrated long before from the island of Scandza in the far north.

events.label: The Wielbark Culture
events.description: The archaeological culture of the lower Vistula associated with the early Goths before their migration south.

events.label: The Goths Migrate to the Black Sea
events.description: Gothic peoples moved south to the Pontic steppe, where they dominated a multiethnic world of farmers and horsemen.

events.label: The Chernyakhov Culture
events.description: A rich material culture spanning modern Ukraine and Romania, blending Gothic, Sarmatian, and provincial-Roman elements.

events.label: Gothic Sea-Raids on the Aegean
events.description: Gothic fleets ravaged the cities of Greece and Asia Minor, including the sack of Athens, in the empire's deepest crisis.

events.label: Battle of Naissus
events.description: The emperor Claudius II won a crushing victory over a vast Gothic host, earning the title Gothicus and a generation of frontier respite.

events.label: Visigoths and Ostrogoths Diverge
events.description: The Goths separated into the Tervingi and Greuthungi, the peoples later remembered as the Visigoths and Ostrogoths.

events.label: Ulfilas Translates the Gothic Bible
events.description: The missionary bishop Ulfilas devised a Gothic alphabet and translated the Bible, making the Goths a literate, Christian people.

events.label: The Gothic Bible
events.description: The earliest substantial text in any Germanic language, surviving most famously in the silver-inked Codex Argenteus.

events.label: The Goths Adopt Arian Christianity
events.description: The Goths embraced the Arian form of Christianity, setting them religiously apart from the Catholic Romans they would rule.

events.label: The Huns Shatter the Greuthungi
events.description: The sudden arrival of the Huns destroyed the Ostrogothic power on the steppe and set the Visigoths fleeing toward Rome.

events.label: Fritigern Leads the Goths Across the Danube
events.description: The emperor Valens admitted desperate Gothic refugees into the empire; Roman mistreatment soon drove them to revolt.

events.label: Battle of Adrianople
events.description: The Goths destroyed the eastern Roman field army and killed the emperor Valens, the worst Roman defeat since Cannae.

events.label: The Gothic Foedus of 382
events.description: Theodosius I settled the Goths inside the empire as autonomous federates, an unprecedented armed nation within Roman borders.

events.label: Alaric Becomes King of the Visigoths
events.description: Alaric united the Gothic federates and turned them into a mobile army bargaining and warring its way across the empire.

events.label: Alaric Sacks Rome
events.description: The Visigoths sacked the city of Rome for three days, an event that shook the Roman world and prompted Augustine's City of God.

events.label: The Visigoths Settle in Aquitaine
events.description: Rome granted the Visigoths land in southern Gaul, where they built the kingdom of Toulouse as nominal imperial allies.

events.label: Visigoths Fight Attila at the Catalaunian Plains
events.description: The Visigothic king Theodoric I died helping the Roman general Aetius halt Attila the Hun in Gaul.

events.label: The Code of Euric
events.description: The Visigothic king Euric issued one of the earliest written Germanic law codes, blending Gothic custom with Roman law.

events.label: Battle of Vouillé
events.description: Clovis and the Franks defeated and killed the Visigothic king Alaric II, driving the Visigoths south into Hispania.

events.label: The Visigothic Kingdom of Toledo
events.description: The Visigoths re-centered their kingdom on the Iberian Peninsula with its capital at Toledo.

events.label: Odoacer Deposes the Last Western Emperor
events.description: The Germanic commander Odoacer removed Romulus Augustulus and ruled Italy as king, ending the Western Roman line.

events.label: Theodoric the Great Conquers Italy
events.description: The Ostrogothic king Theodoric defeated and killed Odoacer and established a Gothic-Roman kingdom ruled from Ravenna.

events.label: Theodoric's Ravenna
events.description: Theodoric adorned his capital with churches and his own monumental mausoleum, fusing Gothic power with Roman splendor.

events.label: Cassiodorus Serves the Gothic Court
events.description: The Roman senator Cassiodorus administered and chronicled the Ostrogothic state, later preserving classical learning in his monastery.

events.label: Boethius Executed by Theodoric
events.description: The philosopher Boethius, accused of treason, wrote the Consolation of Philosophy in prison before his execution.

events.label: Justinian Launches the Gothic War
events.description: The Eastern emperor Justinian sent armies to reconquer Italy from the Ostrogoths, beginning a devastating twenty-year war.

events.label: Belisarius Takes Rome and Ravenna
events.description: Justinian's general Belisarius captured the Gothic capitals, seeming to end the war before a Gothic revival reversed it.

events.label: Totila's Gothic Revival
events.description: The energetic king Totila reconquered most of Italy and held it for a decade against the Eastern Empire.

events.label: Battle of Taginae
events.description: The eunuch general Narses defeated and killed Totila, breaking Ostrogothic resistance for good.

events.label: The End of Ostrogothic Italy
events.description: Justinian's Pragmatic Sanction reimposed imperial rule over a ruined Italy, closing the Gothic chapter of the West.

events.label: The Gothic Black Sea Trade
events.description: From the Pontic steppe the Goths controlled grain, slaves, and Roman goods moving between the empire and the barbarian interior.

events.label: Gothic Polychrome Metalwork
events.description: Gothic smiths perfected garnet cloisonné goldwork, an elite craft technique that spread across the migrating Germanic world.
